project.title: Music of Asian Americans
project.description:
This advanced seminar follows Asian and Asian-diasporic sound across the
Americas from the twentieth century to the present. We listen in rehearsal
rooms and jazz labs, punk basements and club nights, film soundstages and
gallery installations. Our focus is less on passport categories than on how
artists shape experience across media — records and scores, screens and
stages, everyday platforms and public space. Our lens is comparative and
relational: we read Asian and diasporic cases alongside broader contexts so
that students from any background can translate methods and insights
across musical cultures.

Each week turns on a theme that cuts across region and genre: Time,
Memory, Space, Perspective, Ecology, Spectacle, Movement, Machines, Belonging, Ritual.
We study composers, bands, producers, choreographers, filmmakers, and
sound artists whose work circulates through U.S., Canadian, Latin
American, and Caribbean contexts. Case studies span big-band
experiments, hip-hop and R&B storytelling, experimental electronics,
diasporic pop infrastructures, and performance works sited in theaters,
museums, and city streets.

Work in the course centers on a running Portfolio: short analytical pieces,
close-listening notes, micro-fieldwork, and audio/visual recordings. Students
may channel their ideas into artistic responses such as sound pieces,
performance scores, experimental multimedia, or hybrid projects that pair
argument with making. We’ll draw on campus and local resources for live
and recorded examples and, when possible, fold nearby events into our
exploration.

No prior musical training required — just open ears, steady curiosity, and a
willingness to work across forms. Check Canvas for weekly reminders,
readings, audio/visual resources, portfolio prompts, and assessments.
project.creator: raymondhyser
